name: Jim Cullum, Jr.
abstract: James Albert Cullum Jr., better known as Jim Cullum Jr. (September 20, 1941 – August 11, 2019), was an American jazz cornetist known for his contributions to Dixieland jazz.

interviews.title: Jim Cullum interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

works.t: Chanson du cœur brisé (The Song of Songs)

about_quotes.speaker: Allan Vaché
about_quotes.evidence: I worked with a band working in a night club six nights a week and traveling, the Jim Cullum Jazz Band in San Antonio. So I was with them for 17 years.
about_quotes.doc_title: Allan Vaché interviewed by Monk Rowe, Scottsdale, Arizona, April 15, 2000

about_quotes.speaker: Donn Andre
about_quotes.evidence: But I have played with him a couple of times when he showed up in Washington and came and sat in for a couple of numbers.
about_quotes.doc_title: Donn Andre interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, June 6, 2009

about_quotes.speaker: Evan Christopher
about_quotes.evidence: It was for the Jim Cullum Jazz Band. And it was being able to work six nights a week...
about_quotes.doc_title: Evan Christopher interviewed by Monk Rowe, Clinton, New York, October 1, 2009

about_quotes.speaker: Leslie Johnson
about_quotes.evidence: The interviewee does not mention Jim Cullum; the interviewer mentions him as a fact ('Jim Cullum made a big hit last year') to prompt a question about the concert lineup.
about_quotes.doc_title: Leslie Johnson interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 12, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Hal Smith
about_quotes.evidence: Hal has played behind many stars of mainstream jazz, including Bob Wilber, Doc Cheatham, Jim Cullum and Milt Hinton.
about_quotes.doc_title: Hal Smith interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Victor Moore
about_quotes.evidence: He asked me to come over, and I went over... We went over to his club after the thing was over
